meta.notes: Novel AI-generated PSBB practice questions for the SpeedyCAT proof-of-concept. NOT real MCAT/AAMC content. Text-only. Chunk 8 of 10.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes nonmaterial culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cultural change that results when two cultures come into continuous firsthand contact.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The perception of being worse off compared with the others one uses as a reference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A condition of normlessness in which social norms are weak, unclear, or conflicting.

questions.explanation: Nonmaterial culture is best described as the intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of assimilation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The smartphones, cars, and clothing produced and used within a society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Attending the opera and formal galas associated with a society's upper class.

questions.explanation: An example of assimilation is: immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade. In general, assimilation refers to the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: a group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: cultural innovation

choices.label: B
choices.text: cultural relativism

choices.label: C
choices.text: a subculture

choices.label: D
choices.text: stigma

questions.explanation: This description matches a subculture, which is a group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes folkways?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An organized, sustained effort by many people to promote or resist social change.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Informal everyday norms whose violation brings mild disapproval rather than serious punishment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patterns or traits found in all known human cultures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A condition of normlessness in which social norms are weak, unclear, or conflicting.

questions.explanation: Folkways is best described as informal everyday norms whose violation brings mild disapproval rather than serious punishment. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of differential association?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The same coffee chain and phone brand appearing in cities on every continent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A youth learning shoplifting methods and attitudes from a delinquent peer group.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A large coordinated campaign pressing lawmakers for new environmental protections.

questions.explanation: An example of differential association is: a youth learning shoplifting methods and attitudes from a delinquent peer group. In general, differential association refers to the theory that deviance is learned through interaction with others who model and reward it. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of globalization?

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes role strain?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a primary group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A tight-knit family or a lifelong circle of close friends.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A doctor's status carrying roles toward patients, nurses, and administrators at once.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A large city where most interactions are brief, practical, and goal-driven.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A manager torn between being a supportive mentor and a strict enforcer of rules.

questions.explanation: An example of a primary group is: a tight-knit family or a lifelong circle of close friends. In general, a primary group refers to a small group marked by close, personal, and enduring relationships. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of McDonaldization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A clinic redesigned for speed, standardized steps, and measurable throughput like a fast-food counter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A member-run union whose real power gradually concentrates in a few long-serving leaders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A married couple making decisions together as a pair.

questions.explanation: An example of McDonaldization is: a clinic redesigned for speed, standardized steps, and measurable throughput like a fast-food counter. questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of an action potential?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transmitter released at the junction that triggers skeletal muscle contraction.

choices.label: B

choices.text: The all-or-nothing electrical spike that sweeps along a neuron's axon.

questions.explanation: An example of an action potential is: the all-or-nothing electrical spike that sweeps along a neuron's axon. In general, an action potential refers to a brief electrical impulse that travels down an axon when a neuron fires. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the foot-in-the-door technique?
